title: Evaluating Imbalanced Models
concept: Why accuracy fails completely on imbalanced data, and why we must use Precision, Recall, and F1-Score instead.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main benefit of using class weights?

options.text: They balance the model's focus during training without changing the raw dataset.
They double the size of the training dataset.

exercises.explain: Correct! Class weights adjust the mistake penalty math, keeping the original data intact.

cards.title: The smoke detector
cards.body: If a smoke detector never goes off, it is "accurate" 99.9% of the days (when there is no fire). But on the one day there is a fire, it fails. We don't care about its daily accuracy; we care if it rings when there is smoke!

cards.title: Precision and Recall
cards.body:
To evaluate imbalanced models, we use three key metrics:
- Precision: Out of all items the model flagged, how many were actually correct?
- Recall: Out of all the real rare items, how many did the model manage to find?
- F1-Score: The balance between Precision and Recall.

cards.title: The Fishing Net
cards.body: Imagine fishing. Recall is the percentage of all fish in the lake that you caught in your net. Precision is the percentage of things in your net that are actually fish (not seaweed or boots).

cards.title: Evaluating Spam Filters
cards.body: In a spam filter, high Precision means when the model says "Spam," it is almost always right (no important emails get lost). High Recall means the model catches almost all spam emails (your inbox stays clean).

cards.title: Accuracy is always the best metric
cards.body: If a disease affects 1 in 10,000 people, a model that says "Healthy" to everyone is 99.99% accurate. But its Recall is 0%! Accuracy completely masks this catastrophic failure.

cards.title: Precision vs. Recall
cards.body: How we balance accuracy in spotting rare items:

left.text:
Quality of positive alerts.
How many flagged items are actually correct?
(Avoids false alarms)

right.text:
Quantity of positive alerts.
How many of the real cases did we catch?
(Avoids missing things)

exercises.prompt: What does a Recall of 100% mean for a medical tumor detector?

options.text: The model successfully caught every single tumor in the dataset.
The model never made a false alarm.
The model is 100% accurate on healthy scans.
The model used SMOTE to generate tumors.

exercises.explain: Recall measures the proportion of actual positive cases (tumors) that were correctly identified. 100% Recall means none were missed.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a model has high Precision, it means it rarely triggers false alarms.
exercises.explain: True. Precision measures how reliable the model's positive flags are. High precision means when it flags something, it is usually correct.

exercises.sentence: The ___ is a single score that balances both Precision and Recall.
exercises.explain: The F1-Score combines Precision and Recall into a single metric to measure overall performance on imbalanced data.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a situation where Recall is the most important metric?

options.text: A fire alarm system where missing a single fire could be fatal.
A video recommendation system where it is okay to show a bad suggestion occasionally.
A model predicting if a house price will go up or down next year.

exercises.explain: In high-stakes situations like fire detection, missing a positive case (fire) is disastrous, so high Recall (not missing anything) is critical.

exercises.prompt: You are building an AI to detect fraudulent bank transactions. If a transaction is fraudulent, the bank must freeze it. If your model freezes many normal transactions (many false alarms) but catches every single fraud, how would you describe its metrics?

options.text: High Recall (caught all fraud) but Low Precision (many false alarms).
Low Recall (missed fraud) but High Precision (no false alarms).
High Accuracy but Low F1-Score due to class weights.

exercises.explain: Perfect! The model caught all fraud cases (High Recall) but generated many false alarms (Low Precision). Balancing these two is the core challenge of evaluating models on imbalanced data.
